talk_metadata.talk_title: DPP4 as a novel checkpoint for CD8 T cells
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: we observed that they were having much better outcomes compared to patients who received standard of care
Speaker said: we observed that both in human and mouse, the inhibition of DPP4 increases proliferation rates
Speaker said: we observed an increase in spare mitochondrial respiratory capacity, and we also observed increased glycolysis rates

key_data_gaps: No clinical trial data in GBM patients
No prospective validation of DPP4 inhibition in human glioblastoma

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify GBM patients with high CD8+ T cell DPP4 expression who may benefit from repurposed DPP4 inhibitors.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: we observed that CD8 T cells are the main expressers of DPP4 and that inhibition improves proliferation and cytotoxicity.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate single‑cell RNA‑seq and metabolomic data to predict which tumors have DPP4‑high exhausted CD8 T cells and model responder likelihood.

crispro_opportunity.description: Repurpose FDA‑approved gliptins (e.g., sitagliptin) for GBM by combining with checkpoint blockade or CAR‑T therapy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: we turned into the Medicare data set and performed a retrospective analysis where we looked for the patients who were on DPP4 inhibitors and observed better outcomes.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate drug‑target engagement in the GBM microenvironment and predict synergistic dosing schedules with PD‑1 blockade.

cited_competitors.name: sitagliptin
cited_competitors.context: used as the most common DPP4 inhibitor in retrospective analysis

cited_competitors.name: metformin
cited_competitors.context: used as a drug control showing no survival benefit

unresolved_questions: Will prospective DPP4 inhibition improve overall survival in GBM patients?
What is the impact of DPP4 inhibition on myeloid cell function in the GBM microenvironment?

talk_metadata.talk_title: On-Demand GLUT3 Expression Augments CAR T Cell Metabolic Fitness and Antitumor Efficacy While Preventing Toxicity in Glioblastoma Models
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: stable GLUT3 expression induced severe body weight loss and high serum TNF‑alpha
Speaker said: on‑demand GLUT3 expression prevented toxicity and achieved complete responses
Speaker said: stable GLUT3‑expressing CAR T cells lost CC chemo expression and showed high PD‑1/TIM‑3

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: continuous high‑affinity glucose uptake leads to hyperactivation and terminal differentiation causing weight loss and cytokine storm
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker said: we observed some severe body weight loss in CAR T cells expressing GLUT3

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that continuous GLUT3 expression caused hyperactivation and toxicity, indicating the scaffold is not intrinsically safe
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Pursuing broad GLUT3 expression dilutes therapeutic window and leads to systemic toxicity

key_data_gaps: No human glioblastoma CAR‑T trial data
Long‑term persistence of on‑demand GLUT3 CAR T cells not reported

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a computational switch model to predict optimal timing of GLUT3 induction based on tumor glucose measurements.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: on‑demand GLUT3 expression enabled glucose uptake only after antigen recognition and avoided toxicity.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate real‑time glucose sensor data with CAR‑T activation kinetics to design inducible GLUT3 promoters.

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine on‑demand GLUT3 CAR T cells with checkpoint blockade to further reduce PD‑1/TIM‑3 upregulation.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: stable GLUT3‑expressing CAR T cells showed high PD‑1/TIM‑3 expression, whereas on‑demand did not.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model synergistic effects of metabolic enhancement plus PD‑1 inhibition on T‑cell exhaustion trajectories.

cited_competitors.name: EGFRvIII CAR T
cited_competitors.context: referenced as an example of existing glioblastoma CAR T approaches

unresolved_questions: What intracellular signaling pathways are triggered by chronic GLUT3‑mediated glucose influx?
Can on‑demand GLUT3 be safely translated to human CAR T products?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Targeting A20 to Enhance ACT Potency
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: A20 knockout dramatically improved tumor suppression compared to controls
Speaker said: zinc finger seven mutation showed dramatically improved tumor control and survival
Speaker said: perforin knockout completely reversed the zinc finger seven tumor phenotype

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: A20 loss boosts cytokine production but does not fully restore perforin‑mediated killing without zinc‑finger‑7 targeting
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker said: zinc finger seven targeting greatly enhanced ACT activity, implying that A20 knockout alone was suboptimal

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker showed that precise zinc‑finger‑7 inactivation is required; broader A20 knockout may have off‑target NF‑κB effects
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on base‑editing to introduce a single missense mutation may be challenging for GMP pipelines

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that zinc finger seven Ttex had lower perforin expression yet concluded that perforin release (degranulation) was increased – contradiction between lower intracellular perforin and higher functional killing

key_data_gaps: No in vivo safety data for zinc‑finger‑7 edited T cells
Long‑term persistence of A20‑deficient ACT not reported

crispro_opportunity.description: Rescue A20‑targeted ACTs by focusing on zinc‑finger‑7 editing rather than full knockout.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: zinc finger seven targeting greatly enhanced ACT activity, whereas full A20 knockout was less effective.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict off‑target editing outcomes and model the impact of zinc‑finger‑7 disruption on T‑cell signaling networks.

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine zinc‑finger‑7 edited ACT with RIP3 or caspase‑1 modulators to further boost perforin degranulation.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: RIP3 ablation nearly eliminated the tumor phenotype in zinc finger seven mice, indicating a non‑canonical pathway.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate synergistic effects of dual pathway modulation on degranulation kinetics.

cited_competitors.name: NY‑ESO‑1 TCR T cells
cited_competitors.context: used as a model ACT in melanoma

cited_competitors.name: CD19 CAR T cells
cited_competitors.context: used as a model ACT in B‑cell leukemia

unresolved_questions: What is the precise molecular link between zinc‑finger‑7 loss and RIP3‑caspase‑1 activation?
Does zinc‑finger‑7 editing affect T‑cell memory formation long‑term?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Galectin‑1 and exhausted CD8 T cells
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: galectin‑1 expression increases during T cell exhaustion and that this promotes dysfunction
Speaker said: knockout of galectin‑1 in T cells leads to higher IFN‑γ and TNF‑α production
Speaker said: galectin‑1 binds to LAG‑3 and VISTA with micromolar affinity in a glycan‑dependent manner

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: galectin‑1 engages LAG‑3 and VISTA to enforce exhaustion, a pathway not addressed by current PD‑1/CTLA‑4 blockade

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker demonstrated direct binding with KD ~12‑13 µM (LAG‑3) and ~3 µM (VISTA), suggesting a druggable pocket
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Current checkpoint inhibitors do not target galectin‑1‑mediated co‑inhibition

key_data_gaps: In vivo efficacy of galectin‑1 blockade in tumor models
Potential off‑target effects on other glycan‑binding proteins

crispro_opportunity.description: Use galectin‑1 expression on CD8 T cells as a biomarker to select patients for combination therapy with LAG‑3 or VISTA inhibitors.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: galectin‑1 expression increases during T cell exhaustion and binds LAG‑3/VISTA.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate single‑cell RNA‑seq and glycoproteomics to predict galectin‑1‑high exhausted T‑cell subsets.

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop small‑molecule or antibody blockers of the galectin‑1/LAG‑3/VISTA interaction to complement existing checkpoint blockade.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: galectin‑1 binds LAG‑3 and VISTA with measurable affinity, and this binding is glycan‑dependent.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model the structural interface and prioritize compounds that disrupt the glycan‑dependent interaction.

cited_competitors.name: PD‑1 inhibitors
cited_competitors.context: current standard checkpoint therapy not addressing galectin‑1 mediated inhibition

cited_competitors.name: LAG‑3 inhibitors
cited_competitors.context: mentioned as therapeutic targets that galectin‑1 also binds

unresolved_questions: Does galectin‑1 blockade synergize with PD‑1 or LAG‑3 blockade in vivo?
What intracellular signaling pathways are triggered by galectin‑1 binding to LAG‑3/VISTA?

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard
talk_metadata.talk_title: Understanding compensatory signaling after PTPN2 and PTPN1 blockade
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: STAT3 and STAT1 pathways are the main pathways activated in chronically stimulated T cells with PTPN2 inhibition
Speaker said: SOCS3 upregulation likely mediates down‑regulation of IL‑2/STAT5 signaling
Speaker said: double knockout of PTPN2 and SOCS3 restores STAT5 signaling and reduces exhaustion markers

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: dominant STAT3 activation suppresses IL‑2/STAT5 signaling via SOCS3, limiting T‑cell persistence

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker showed that AC44 induces STAT3 dominance, revealing a mechanistic limitation of the inhibitor
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Single‑target PTPN2 blockade does not fully prevent exhaustion in chronic stimulation settings

key_data_gaps: In vivo tumor efficacy of PTPN2/SOCS3 double knockout T cells
Safety profile of combined PTPN2 and SOCS3 disruption

crispro_opportunity.description: Design combination trials that pair PTPN2 inhibition with SOCS3 modulation to sustain IL‑2/STAT5 signaling.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: double knockout PTPN2 and SOCS3 showed strong levels of IL‑2/STAT5 compared to single knockouts.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model cytokine milieu dynamics and predict optimal dosing schedules for dual inhibition.

crispro_opportunity.description: Target SOCS3 upregulation as a resistance node to PTPN2‑based metabolic reprogramming.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: SOCS3 is a known inhibitor of JAK‑STAT pathway and is induced in PTPN2‑inhibited T cells.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate SOCS3 knockdown effects on STAT5 phosphorylation kinetics.

cited_competitors.name: AC44 (PTPN2 inhibitor)
cited_competitors.context: used to inhibit PTPN2 in chronic stimulation assays

unresolved_questions: Will SOCS3 knockout in human T cells be safe and maintain controlled cytokine release?
How does the STAT3‑dominant program affect long‑term memory formation?
